raw_case:
Rare case of thigh lipoblastoma in an infant: A case report


Introduction and importance

Lipoblastoma is a rare, benign tumor that primarily affects infants and young children, representing a small percentage of soft tissue tumors in this population. Its early diagnosis and surgical management are crucial for favorable outcomes, as lipoblastoma can lead to significant complications if left untreated.

Case presentation

We report the case of an 18-month-old female toddler who presented with a progressively growing, painless mass in the left medial thigh, which had developed over six months. Imaging studies, including CT and MRI, revealed a well-defined, hypodense lobulated mass consistent with lipoblastoma. Surgical excision was performed, and the mass measured approximately 7 by 6 cm. Histopathological examination confirmed the diagnosis of lipoblastoma. The patient experienced a successful recovery without recurrence during a one-year follow-up period.

Discussion

Lipoblastoma typically presents as a painless subcutaneous soft tissue mass that progressively grows. Symptoms often relate to the tumor's location and size or the mass effect on surrounding structures. Imaging studies, particularly CT and MRI, play a crucial role in making a presumptive diagnosis by revealing the tumor's characteristics and components. Total excision remains the treatment of choice for lipoblastoma.

Conclusion

Lipoblastoma should be included in the differential diagnosis of rapidly growing, painless masses in children under three years of age. The treatment of choice is complete surgical resection of the tumor, which is crucial for achieving favorable outcomes and reducing recurrence risk.

Highlights

•Lipoblastoma is a rare benign mesenchymal tumor of the infant and early childhood.•Lipoblastoma should be suspected in case of heterogeneous fatty tumor in children younger than 3 years.•Complete no mutilating resection of the tumor is the treatment of choice.•The prognosis is excellent, with a recurrence rate less than 25% being largely attributed to incomplete resection1

Methods

This case report is conducted following the SCARE criteria for surgical case reports [1].2

Introduction

Lipoblastoma is a rare, benign neoplasm derived from embryonic white fat, primarily affecting infants and young children. They account for approximately 4 % to 6 % of soft-tissue tumors in this population, with a slight male predominance [2,3]. These tumors are characterized by rapid growth and can vary in symptoms based on their location, often causing deformities or compression of adjacent structures. Clinically, lipoblastomas typically present as painless masses, most commonly located in the extremities, although they can also occur in the head and neck region [3].There are two clinical variants: lipoblastoma, which is encapsulated and superficial, accounting for about 70 % of cases, and lipoblastomatosis, which infiltrates surrounding muscle tissue. Both variants share similar histological features [2].Diagnosis is often determined by clinical and radiological findings, particularly through magnetic resonance imaging (MRI). However, definitive diagnosis relies on histological examination, which reveals well-defined lobules of adipocytes, uniform differentiation, and the absence of atypical cells and significant lipoblasts [4,5]. Here, we report a case of a left thigh lipoblastoma in an 18-month-old female toddler, highlighting its clinical presentation, imaging characteristics, and the importance of differential diagnosis in pediatric cases.3

Case presentation

An 18-month-old female child presented with a gradual enlargement of her left medial thigh over the past six months. The mass had been growing painlessly, without any history of trauma or infection. Upon physical examination, a firm, painless, non-pulsatile mass measuring approximately 10 cm was noted in the proximal thigh, fixed to the underlying tissues yet movable relative to the overlying skin. (Fig. 1a & b) There were no signs of inflammation, neurovascular impairment, or inguinal adenopathy.A CT scan revealed a well-defined, hypodense lobulated mass in the left anteromedial compartment of the proximal thigh, extending to the inguinal area. The mass exhibited internal septation with a density comparable to subcutaneous fat, displacing the common and superficial femoral vessels anterolaterally without signs of invasion. The mass also abuts proximal femoral bone but no lytic or sclerotic lesion seen (Fig. 2a & b). An MRI confirmed the presence of a fatty-appearing mass with similar characteristics, showing no encasement of femoral vessels (Fig. 3).The patient underwent surgical excision of the mass through a left anteromedial thigh incision, with careful preservation of the femoral vessels and nerves. The mass measured approximately 7 by 6 cm and weighed 650 g. It was well-encapsulated, soft, and yellowish-white (Fig. 4). Complete resection was achieved, and the surgical wound was closed in layers with a drain placed for 48 h.Histopathological examination revealed a tumor composed of mature fat, myxoid changes, and lipoblasts among adipocytes. The center of the lesion displayed numerous myxoid changes, plexiform capillary channels, and mature adipocyte differentiation (Fig. 5a-d).The patient had an uneventful recovery and was discharged in good condition. At the one-year follow-up, there were no signs of recurrence as shown in the following figure (Fig. 6).4

Discussion

Lipoblastoma is a rare tumor that arises from embryonic white fat, primarily affecting infants and young children. It is characterized histologically by immature adipocytes, defined septa, numerous lipoblasts, and a fine vascular network. Clinically, lipoblastomas typically present as painless subcutaneous soft tissue masses that progressively grow which may mature into lipomas [4]. Symptoms are often related to the lesion's location, size, and mass effect. The extremities and trunk are the most common locations for these tumors, while the head and neck region accounts for 10 % to 15 % of cases [3]. The pathophysiology of lipoblastoma is thought to result from genetic predisposition and alterations in human white fat embryogenesis. Anomalies on chromosome 8 have been linked to the development of lipoblastoma [4].A critical aspect of diagnosing lipoblastoma is differentiating it from liposarcoma, particularly the myxoid type, which is a malignant tumor [6]. Although liposarcoma is uncommon in individuals younger than ten years old, pathologists may find it challenging to distinguish it from lipoblastoma due to histological similarities. Key indicators that suggest liposarcoma include the absence of lobulation, varied growth patterns, and elevated nuclear atypia [7].In addition to liposarcoma, other important differential diagnoses for fat-containing tumors include lipoma, teratoma, dermoid cyst, hibernoma, and involuting hemangioma [2]. True lipomas typically share characteristics with subcutaneous fat, but a fat-rich lipoblastoma can mimic this appearance. The clinical history of progressive growth can help distinguish a fat-rich lipoblastoma from a lipoma. Teratomas and dermoid cysts often contain calcifications or cystic areas in addition to fatty components [8]. Involuting hemangiomas exhibit a characteristic growth pattern marked by rapid proliferation during the first few months of life, followed by stabilization around 9 to 10 months, and a gradual involution phase that continues over several years, ultimately resulting in replacement by fibrofatty tissue by around 7 to 10 years of age [9]. Hibernomas typically occur in individuals in their 30s to 40s and are characterized by unique vascular structures [8].Imaging studies, particularly CT and MRI, play a crucial role in presumptive diagnosis by revealing the tumor's characteristics, origin, composition, and anatomical extent, which aids in surgical planning [10].The prognosis for lipoblastoma is generally excellent, with a recurrence rate of less than 25 %. Most recurrence cases are attributed to incomplete resection of large lesions or muscle infiltration. Recurrence is notably higher in lipoblastomatosis [11].Given the limited literature on this rare condition, we hope this case provides valuable insights and encourages consideration of lipoblastoma in the differential diagnosis for painless masses in young children. In addition to emphasizing the importance of complete surgical resection, we highlight the need for ongoing monitoring for recurrence to ensure the best possible outcomes.

Author contribution

All authors contributed to the conception, writing and editing of the case report. All authors are agreed to be accountable for all aspects of it.

Consent

Written informed consent was obtained from the patient's parents/legal guardian for publication and any accompanying images. A copy of the written consent is available for review by the Editor-in-Chief of this journal on request.

Ethical approval

According to our institution, ethical clearance is not required for case reports.

Guarantor

Sileshi Serebe Zeleke.

Funding

Not applicable.

Conflict of interest statement

The authors declare that they have no competing interests.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** 18-month-old female toddler.
- **Chief Complaint:** Progressive painless mass in the left medial thigh.
- **History of Present Illness:** Gradual enlargement of the left medial thigh mass over six months without pain, trauma, or infection.
- **Past Medical History:** None.
- **Personal History:** None.
- **Family History:** None.
- **Physical Examination:** Firm, painless, non-pulsatile thigh mass (~10 cm), movable relative to skin, fixed to underlying tissues, no inflammation, neurovascular impairment, or inguinal adenopathy.
- **Ancillary Tests:**  
  - **CT Scan:** Well-defined, hypodense lobulated mass in the left anteromedial thigh with internal septation, displacing femoral vessels without invasion.  
  - **MRI:** Fatty-looking lobulated mass with no vascular encasement.  
  - **Histopathology:** Tumor composed of mature fat, myxoid changes, lipoblasts, plexiform capillary channels, and mature adipocytes. No atypical cells present.
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
- The differential diagnosis began with considering fat-containing tumors common in young children (<3 years).
- **Lipoblastoma:** Likely due to the patient's age, a painless progressively growing mass, imaging studies showing lobulated fat-containing mass, and histological findings of benign adipocytic tissue with lipoblasts.
- **Liposarcoma:** Excluded due to the patient’s age (liposarcomas are rare in children under 10), absence of nuclear atypia, and characteristic histology of uniform differentiation and lobulation.
- **Lipoma:** Excluded due to patient age (lipomas are unusual in very young children) and progressive growth of the mass.
- **Teratoma/Dermoid cyst:** Excluded as no calcifications or cystic areas were observed in imaging.
- **Involuting Hemangioma:** Excluded due to the absence of a proliferative phase followed by stabilization/involution, which defines hemangiomas.
- **Hibernoma:** Excluded due to the patient’s young age (hibernomas are more common in adults).

Definitive diagnosis confirmed as lipoblastoma based on imaging and histopathology findings.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: Lipoblastoma of the left medial thigh.

results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> **Patient Demographics and Chief Complaint Analysis:** An 18-month-old female toddler presents with a progressive, painless thigh mass.  This age group is important as certain tumors are more prevalent in infancy and early childhood. The painless nature and thigh location are initial key features.

<step 2> **History of Present Illness Evaluation:** The six-month gradual, painless growth of the mass without trauma or infection suggests a benign or slow-growing lesion rather than an acute inflammatory or traumatic process. The absence of pain significantly reduces the likelihood of infectious etiologies.

<step 3> **Review of Medical History:** The absence of past medical, personal, and family history is non-contributory in this case, but important to note that there are no obvious predisposing factors mentioned.

<step 4> **Physical Examination Findings Interpretation:** A firm, painless, non-pulsatile, ~10 cm thigh mass, movable relative to the skin but fixed to deeper tissues, is described. Lack of inflammation, neurovascular compromise, and inguinal adenopathy is noted. The size suggests a substantial lesion. Fixation to deeper tissues is a relevant finding that needs to be considered in differential diagnosis but the lack of neurovascular impairment is reassuring regarding aggressiveness. The absence of lymph node involvement (inguinal adenopathy) makes metastatic or infectious etiology less likely.

<step 5> **Ancillary Tests - CT Scan Interpretation:** The CT describes a well-defined, hypodense, lobulated mass with internal septation, displacing but not invading femoral vessels. "Hypodense" in CT typically indicates fat or fluid. "Well-defined" and "non-invasive displacement" are generally suggestive of a benign or low-grade lesion.  The lobulated nature and internal septation are descriptive features.

<step 6> **Ancillary Tests - MRI Interpretation:**  The MRI confirms a "fatty-looking" lobulated mass without vascular encasement. This significantly strengthens the likelihood of a lipomatous tumor. The absence of vascular encasement is a favorable finding, suggesting non-aggressive behavior.

<step 7> **Ancillary Tests - Histopathology Interpretation:** The histopathology report is crucial. It describes a tumor composed of mature fat, myxoid changes, lipoblasts, plexiform capillary channels, and mature adipocytes, with *no atypical cells*. This histological description is highly characteristic of a benign lipomatous tumor, specifically pointing towards Lipoblastoma. The presence of mature fat and adipocytes confirms the lipomatous nature. Myxoid changes, lipoblasts, and plexiform capillary channels are features often seen in lipoblastomas. The critical finding is the absence of atypical cells, which strongly favors a benign diagnosis over a malignant one like liposarcoma.

<step 8> **Differential Diagnosis and Final Diagnosis:** Based on the age, clinical presentation (painless mass), imaging (fatty mass), and most importantly, the histopathology report (mature fat, lipoblasts, no atypia), the most likely diagnosis is **Lipoblastoma**.  Lipoblastoma is a benign tumor of embryonal fat that typically occurs in infants and young children, fitting the patient's age. The histological features described are classic for lipoblastoma. While lipoma could be considered, lipoblastoma is more common in this age group and better fits the histological description provided, particularly the presence of lipoblasts and plexiform capillary channels. Liposarcoma is highly unlikely given the benign histopathology report explicitly stating "no atypical cells".

### Answer:
Lipoblastoma
